facts.label: BIRTH OF LA CELESTE
facts.story: In 1910, Uruguay didn't yet have a signature color. Federation official Ricardo LeBas proposed a sky-blue jersey, honoring River Plate's famous win over Alumni, and association president Héctor Gómez approved it. On August 15, 1910, Uruguay wore the light blue for the first time against Argentina and won 3–1 in Montevideo. The result and the shirt fused into legend, and Uruguayans have called their team La Celeste — 'the sky blue one' — ever since.

facts.label: FOUR STARS, ONE FIGHT
facts.story: Uruguay's crest carries four gold stars, not two — a nod to FIFA-sanctioned world titles in the 1924 and 1928 Olympics as well as 1930 and 1950. FIFA approved the extra stars in 1992 after historian Atilio Garrido dug up federation documents from the 1920s. In 2021, a leaked email revealed FIFA had quietly asked kit maker Puma to strip two stars before a World Cup qualifier. Uruguay's federation mobilized historians and archives in response, FIFA backed down, and the four stars stayed for the 2022 World Cup jersey.

1930.headline: Castro Seals It At Home
1930.story: Down 2–1 at halftime in the inaugural final, Uruguay leaned on 90,000 fans at the brand-new Estadio Centenario. Pedro Cea equalized, Santos Iriarte put them ahead in the 68th minute, and one-armed forward Héctor Castro headed home in the final minute to beat Argentina 4–2. The following day was declared a national holiday.

annotations.label: THE DIVINE ONE-ARMED
annotations.story: Héctor Castro lost his right forearm in a childhood accident with an electric saw, yet nobody told his marker in the 89th minute. Rising above a tiring Argentina backline, 'El Divino Manco' — the Divine One-Armed — met a cross with his head and buried the winner that made Montevideo erupt.

annotations.label: UNBRANDED AND HANDMADE
annotations.story: Long before crests carried stars or shirts carried logos, Uruguay's players pulled on plain celeste cotton jerseys stitched by hand, paired with black shorts and socks — colors chosen two decades earlier after a win over Argentina's Alumni club. No manufacturer's mark appeared anywhere; the federation, not a company, supplied the kit.

annotations.label: BUILT IN EIGHT MONTHS
annotations.story: Estadio Centenario rose from bare ground in barely eight months, its concrete still curing as fans filed in. Only four European federations made the three-week transatlantic crossing to compete, leaving the inaugural tournament overwhelmingly American — a snub that stung organizers even as Uruguay, fresh off consecutive Olympic titles, romped to the trophy.

1930.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

matches.scorers: Cea 18', Anselmo 20', Anselmo 31', Iriarte 61', Cea 67', Cea 72'

1950.headline: Silencing The Maracanã
1950.story: Trailing Brazil's daunting home run and needing only a draw to hand the hosts the title, Uruguay went scoreless into the second half of the decisive match. Juan Alberto Schiaffino equalized, and with 11 minutes left, Alcides Ghiggia beat the goalkeeper at his near post to silence 173,000 fans inside the Maracanã, sealing Uruguay's second title 2–1.

annotations.label: THE POPE AND SINATRA
annotations.story: Alcides Ghiggia later joked that only three people had ever silenced the Maracanã: the Pope, Frank Sinatra, and him. His low finish at the near post in the 79th minute did exactly that to 200,000 stunned Brazilians, turning a coronation into the most famous silence in football history.

annotations.label: NO FIXED NUMBERS YET
annotations.story: Uruguay's celeste jersey in 1950 carried a plain white ribbed collar and a modest embroidered badge — no gold stars, no sponsor. Squad numbers weren't yet mandatory or permanent across a tournament; that rule, tying one man to one digit for all matches, wouldn't arrive until the following World Cup in 1954.

annotations.label: HEADLINES PRINTED TOO SOON
annotations.story: Rio's O Mundo printed a pre-match edition captioned 'these are the world champions' beside a photo of Brazil's squad. Uruguay's captain Obdulio Varela bought up copies, spread them across a bathroom floor, and had teammates walk over them — fuel for the upset that followed hours later at the Maracanã.

1950.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

matches.scorers: Míguez 14', Vidal 18', Schiaffino 23', Míguez 40', Míguez 51', Schiaffino 54', Pérez 83', Ghiggia 87'

1954.headline: The Unbeaten Run Ends
1954.story: Uruguay hadn't lost a single World Cup match in 24 years before Hungary's 'Golden Team' finally beat them 4–2 after extra time in a raging Basel semi-final. Juan Hohberg forced extra time with a stunning equalizer, but Sándor Kocsis's two late goals ended the run. A weary Uruguay then lost the third-place match to Austria 3–1.

annotations.label: COLLAPSE AND KEEP PLAYING
annotations.story: Juan Hohberg leveled Hungary at 2-2 in the 87th minute, then collapsed face-first in his teammates' celebratory pile-up, apparently from a heart episode. Trainers revived him on the sideline; ignoring advice to stop, he played all of extra time, but Sándor Kocsis's late double still ended Uruguay's 24-year unbeaten run 4–2.

annotations.label: NUMBERS FINALLY FIXED
annotations.story: Switzerland 1954 was the first World Cup where each Uruguayan wore one number for the whole tournament, 1 through 22, rather than shirts reshuffled match to match. It was a small bureaucratic shift that made the celeste shirt, still plain and starless, feel for the first time like a real uniform rather than a costume.

annotations.label: TWENTY-FOUR YEARS UNBEATEN
annotations.story: Uruguay arrived in Switzerland having never lost a single World Cup match across three prior tournaments, a run stretching back to the first ball kicked in 1930. Basel's semi-final against Hungary's 'Golden Team' — unbeaten for four years themselves — was less a game than a collision of two invincibility myths, and only one survived.

1954.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

matches.scorers: Borges 17', Míguez 30', Borges 47', Abbadie 54', Borges 57', Míguez 83', Abbadie 85'

1962.headline: Worst Finish In Chile
1962.story: A win over Colombia couldn't save Uruguay from a rough Group Stage in windswept Chile. Losses to Yugoslavia and the Soviet Union, by a combined 2–5, sent them home after the first round, one of the more forgettable showings of the golden generation's fading years.

annotations.label: A LONE BRIGHT SPOT
annotations.story: Luis Cubilla and Domingo Sasía scored 19 minutes apart to see off Colombia 2–1 in Arica, a desert port city near the Peruvian border chosen as one of four World Cup host venues. It proved the only Uruguayan win of the tournament before back-to-back defeats sent the golden generation home early.

annotations.label: A HOMEMADE SHIRT
annotations.story: No foreign manufacturer touched Uruguay's kit in 1962 — the celeste jersey was produced domestically, still years from the crest's four stars or any sponsor logo. The shirt's simplicity mirrored a squad in transition, its 1950 heroes retired or aging, with only a handful of survivors from the golden era still on the field.

annotations.label: A HOST STILL REBUILDING
annotations.story: Chile staged the tournament barely two years after the largest earthquake ever recorded, a 9.5-magnitude quake that flattened stadium towns. Organizer Carlos Dittborn's rallying cry — 'because we have nothing, we will do everything' — became the tournament's slogan; he died a month before kickoff and never saw Uruguay's brief, forgettable stay in his rebuilt country.

1962.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

1966.headline: Nine Men, Wembley Injustice
1966.story: Stubborn draws with England and Mexico carried Uruguay through the group unbeaten. In the quarter-final, referee Jim Finney waved off a blatant Karl-Heinz Schnellinger handball on the goal line, then sent off Horacio Troche and Héctor Silva within ten minutes of the second half. West Germany punished the nine men, scoring three late goals in a 4–0 rout.

annotations.label: THE DOG TRACK WIN
annotations.story: Uruguay's group opener against France wasn't played at Wembley at all — it moved to White City Stadium because the arena's owners wouldn't cancel a greyhound race booked for that evening. Uruguay won 2–1 among the dog track's floodlights, a strange staging note before the tournament's real controversy arrived in the quarter-final.

annotations.label: BRANDED NEGATIVE BY PRESS
annotations.story: Jimmy Greaves complained that 'Uruguay's negative tactics soon choked the life out of the game' after the goalless draw with England, and English papers piled on with words like 'dour' and 'frustrating.' The defensive reputation stuck even as Uruguay advanced unbeaten from the group, undefeated until West Germany's quarter-final ambush.

annotations.label: STILL HOME-PRODUCED
annotations.story: By 1966, most European sides had signed with outside kit makers, but Uruguay's celeste jersey remained an in-house production, plain and unbranded. The shirt carried no stars, no sponsor, nothing but the small national crest — a holdover from an amateur-adjacent era just before Adidas and Puma began dressing entire federations.

1966.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

1970.headline: The Disputed Ghost Goal
1970.story: Ladislao Mazurkiewicz anchored tight defending as Uruguay ground through the group stage, then Víctor Espárrago settled a brutal, foul-riddled quarter-final against the Soviet Union with a 117th-minute winner the Soviets swore never should have counted. Brazil's eventual champions ended the run in the semi-final 3–1, and West Germany beat Uruguay again in the third-place match.

annotations.label: PELÉ'S FAMOUS DUMMY
annotations.story: In the semi-final, Pelé received a long ball with Ladislao Mazurkiewicz rushing out, let it run past both himself and the goalkeeper without touching it, then curled his shot just wide from an impossible angle. Even in defeat, Uruguay's part in one of football's most replayed moments outlived the 3–1 scoreline.

annotations.label: A DARKER CELESTE
annotations.story: Local outfitter Sanz Hermanos supplied Uruguay's 1970 shirt, a more tailored cut than the boxy jerseys of the 1950s, in a deeper, more saturated celeste blue. It was among the last kits Uruguay wore before international sportswear brands like Adidas took over supply later in the decade.

annotations.label: GUERRILLA WAR BACK HOME
annotations.story: While the team chased a fifth World Cup semi-final in Mexico, the Tupamaros urban guerrilla movement was nearing the peak of its campaign back in Montevideo, kidnapping officials and rattling a fragile government. Uruguayan football offered a rare unifying distraction from a country sliding toward the dictatorship that would follow within four years.

1970.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

1974.headline: A Quiet European Exit
1974.story: A rebuilding Uruguay, missing the golden generation's old guard, managed only a draw with Bulgaria and losses to the Netherlands and Sweden in West Germany. Johan Cruyff's soon-to-be-legendary Dutch side beat them 2–0, and Uruguay finished bottom of their group without scoring more than a single goal all tournament.

annotations.label: TOTAL FOOTBALL ARRIVES
annotations.story: Johan Cruyff's Netherlands used their opening match against Uruguay to showcase what the world would soon call Total Football on its biggest stage — fluid, interchanging positions that no defense had prepared for. Uruguay lost 2–0 and never solved the puzzle, a preview of the tactical revolution that would carry the Dutch to the final.

annotations.label: ADIDAS TAKES OVER
annotations.story: West Germany 1974 marked Uruguay's first tournament dressed by Adidas, beginning an eight-year kit-supply relationship. The switch to a global manufacturer brought Uruguay's celeste jersey in line with the three-stripe template sweeping international football, even as the team on the field was a rebuilding side with none of 1950's stars left.

annotations.label: A LONG ABSENCE BEGINS
annotations.story: This quiet, low-scoring exit was the last Uruguay would manage for over a decade — after 1974, the celeste failed to qualify for either 1978 or 1982, their longest World Cup drought. It would take twelve years and a new generation to bring them back, at Mexico 1986.

1974.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

1986.headline: Fastest Red Card Ever
1986.story: José Batista was sent off just 56 seconds into the Scotland match, still the fastest red card in World Cup history, yet ten men held out for a scoreless draw. A 6–1 thrashing by Denmark was the low point before Diego Maradona's Argentina eliminated them 1–0 in the Round of 16.

annotations.label: PASCULLI ENDS THE RUN
annotations.story: Maradona called this his best individual display of the tournament without even scoring — his midfield dummy sprung Sergio Batista forward, the ball found Jorge Burruchaga, and a deflected cross fell for Pedro Pasculli to finish just before halftime. That 42nd-minute strike was enough; Uruguay never found an equalizer against Argentina.

annotations.label: FRENCH STRIPES ON CELESTE
annotations.story: Le Coq Sportif supplied Uruguay's kit for Mexico 1986, its final year before Puma took over the contract. The French brand's signature rooster logo sat beside the plain, still starless crest — a quietly odd pairing of a French sportswear brand and South America's oldest football jersey.

annotations.label: SCUM OF WORLD FOOTBALL
annotations.story: Uruguay's disciplinary record in Mexico — four bookings and a sending-off against Denmark alone, then Batista's 56-second red against Scotland — provoked fury. Scotland manager Alex Ferguson said he was 'glad to be going home,' and the Scottish FA's Ernie Walker branded them 'the scum of world football,' a reputation coach Omar Borrás never fully shook.

1986.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

1990.headline: Rome Ends Their Run
1990.story: Uruguay stumbled through the group stage, needing a late Daniel Fonseca goal to beat South Korea and sneak through in third place. Italy, the host nation riding a wave of momentum, shut them out 2–0 in Rome's Round of 16 in front of a hostile crowd.

annotations.label: SCHILLACI BREAKS THE DEADLOCK
annotations.story: A scoreless first half in Rome's Olimpico gave Uruguay hope, but Salvatore Schillaci — Italy's surprise breakout scorer all tournament — broke through in the 65th minute, and Aldo Serena doubled it in the 85th. Uruguay never mustered a reply in front of a crowd solidly behind the hosts.

annotations.label: PUMA DRESSES THE CELESTE
annotations.story: Puma had supplied Uruguay's kit since 1987, and Italia '90 marked the brand's only World Cup appearance with the team before another switch mid-decade. The celeste shirt stayed plain — still no stars on the crest, two years before FIFA would recognize Uruguay's Olympic titles and let the badge finally reflect four championships instead of two.

annotations.label: A YOUNG DEMOCRACY'S TEAM
annotations.story: This squad represented a Uruguay barely five years removed from twelve years of military dictatorship, which had ended in 1985 with the return of elected government. Football carried outsized weight in that transition — a rare source of unified national pride while the country still worked through the wounds of the era.

1990.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

2002.headline: A Long-Awaited Return
2002.story: After missing two straight tournaments, Uruguay returned to find Group A brutal. A 1–2 loss to Denmark started things off, and second-half comebacks salvaged draws with France and Senegal, including a wild 3–3 draw with the African debutants. It wasn't enough — Uruguay finished third, well outside the knockout places.

annotations.label: THREE DOWN, THREE BACK
annotations.story: Uruguay trailed African debutants Senegal 3–0 inside 40 minutes in the group finale, undone by a penalty and a Papa Bouba Diop strike. Richard Morales, Diego Forlán's chest-and-volley wonder goal, and an Álvaro Recoba penalty in the 88th minute completed one of the great World Cup comebacks — for a draw that still wasn't enough to advance.

annotations.label: A LOCAL LABEL
annotations.story: Rather than a global sportswear giant, Uruguay's 2002 shirt was produced by L-Sporto, a comparatively small outfitter — a modest arrangement for a country's first World Cup jersey of the new millennium, before Puma took over the kit deal a few years later.

annotations.label: A COUNTRY ON EDGE
annotations.story: Uruguay's players competed in Korea and Japan as their economy crept toward a full banking collapse back home — a crisis triggered by Argentina's 2001 meltdown that would freeze deposits and wipe out a third of the country's savings within weeks of the tournament ending. Football offered little relief once qualification for the knockouts slipped away.

2002.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

2010.headline: Suárez's Handball Saves Them
2010.story: Diego Forlán's brilliance carried Uruguay through a golden run, but the quarter-final against Ghana turned on Luis Suárez's deliberate handball on the line in extra time, saving a certain goal. Asamoah Gyan missed the resulting penalty, and Uruguay won the shootout 4–2. The Netherlands ended the dream run in the semi-final, 3–2.

annotations.label: FORLÁN'S THUNDERBOLT
annotations.story: Down 1-0 to the Netherlands in the semi-final, Diego Forlán leveled with a left-footed strike from 25 yards that flew dead center past the keeper. It was the emotional high point of a tournament that later crowned him the Golden Ball winner as the competition's best player, even in a losing cause.

annotations.label: FANS FORCE A REDESIGN
annotations.story: Puma's original away kit for South Africa was gold, a design fans of 'La Celeste' revolted against as a betrayal of the team's sky-blue identity. Puma and the federation scrapped it and released a white shirt with light-blue panels arcing from the underarms to the hem instead, a rare case of supporters winning a kit fight.

annotations.label: HERO HOME, VILLAIN ABROAD
annotations.story: Luis Suárez's goal-line handball against Ghana turned him into a reviled figure across the rest of the continent and a folk hero back home, mobbed by fans on his return to Montevideo. The same instant produced opposite verdicts on either side of the Atlantic — pure villainy in Accra, calculated heroism in Uruguay.

2010.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

2014.headline: The Bite Heard Round the World
2014.story: Luis Suárez sank his teeth into Giorgio Chiellini's shoulder in the group finale against Italy, a moment that overshadowed his two goals against England days earlier and got him banned before the Round of 16. Without him, James Rodríguez's Colombia eliminated a toothless Uruguay 2–0.
2014.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks

2018.headline: Cavani's Double, France Ends It
2018.story: A perfect group stage, including a 3–0 rout of host Russia, set up a Round of 16 date with Portugal, which Edinson Cavani beat almost single-handedly with two goals. An injured Cavani couldn't finish the quarter-final against France, and Uruguay's best modern run ended 2–0 in Nizhny Novgorod.
2018.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks, four gold stars on crest

2022.headline: Out On A Tiebreaker
2022.story: Giorgian de Arrascaeta's brace fueled a routine 2–0 win over Ghana in the finale, and Uruguay finished level on points and goal difference with South Korea. But Son Heung-min's side had scored two more goals across the group, and Uruguay were eliminated on the tiebreaker after their sole loss came against Portugal.
2022.homeColors: light blue (celeste) shirt, black shorts, black socks, four gold stars on crest
2022.awayColors: white shirt with a large sky-blue fold graphic and light blue vertical stripes
